title: Geometry and Arithmetic - Curves of genus one
speaker: Andrew Wiles

proof_stages.label: Solvable-points problem for curves
proof_stages.summary: The talk recasts solvability by radicals and solvable Galois extensions as the question whether a smooth projective curve over Q has a point over some finite solvable extension, reviews surrounding low-genus and non-number-field behavior, and isolates genus one as the case to attack.

proof_stages.label: Heegner/CM construction of elliptic-curve points
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews Mordell-Weil, BSD, modular parametrizations, and the Gross-Zagier Heegner-point construction, emphasizing that CM points are the main general source of infinite-order points when the relevant analytic rank is one.

proof_stages.label: Cornut-Vatsal nonvanishing in anticyclotomic towers
proof_stages.summary: The argument then replaces a single Heegner point by families over ring class fields in an anticyclotomic Z_p-extension, using the Cornut-Vatsal theorem and its Ratner-theoretic input to ensure non-torsion/nonzero traces for large conductor.

proof_stages.label: Genus-one curves as cohomology classes of their Jacobians
proof_stages.summary: Returning to a genus-one curve C with Jacobian E, the talk identifies C as a torsor class in H^1(Q,E), with the class vanishing exactly when C has a rational point.

proof_stages.label: Kummer descent criterion for solvable points
proof_stages.summary: The multiplication-by-p exact sequence and its long cohomology sequence reformulate the task as finding, after a solvable extension L/Q, E(L)-points whose Kummer images cover the relevant Galois-invariant cohomology classes.

proof_stages.label: Selmer trapping by ramified CM classes
proof_stages.summary: An analogy with trapping the Hilbert class field inside sufficiently ramified tame extensions motivates using Kolyvagin-style classes supplied by the Cornut-Vatsal method to generate enough of the Selmer group, i.e. the locally soluble torsor classes.

proof_stages.label: Solvable-points theorem for semistable locally soluble genus-one curves
proof_stages.summary: Combining the descent/Selmer reformulation with the supply of CM-derived classes proves that a genus-one curve over Q with points everywhere locally and semistable Jacobian has a point over a solvable extension, with the speaker explaining that the hypotheses should be removable after a solvable totally real base change and that higher genus remains open.

examples.name: Generic polynomial of degree at least five
examples.note: Abel's and Galois's one-variable obstruction is used to generate the analogous question for two-variable equations or curves: must solutions occur over solvable extensions?
examples.engagement_reason: the generic degree-at-least-five polynomial is cited via Abel/Galois as motivation, with no computation or analysis carried out on it

passes.reason: ordinary generic high-degree polynomial family cited as the Abel–Ruffini failure of solvability by radicals

examples.name: Low-genus affirmative cases
examples.note: Curves of genus 0, 2, 3, and 4 illustrate known positive cases, using small-degree maps to P^1 or the genus-four canonical quadric-cubic model to force solvable points.
examples.engagement_reason: the low-genus cases are summarized as known affirmative results with brief indications such as maps to P1 or canonical embeddings, but no case is actually worked through

passes.reason: lower-boundary genus cases cited as positive solvable-point results
The genus 0,2,3,4 cases are low-complexity base cases with special low-degree maps to P1, used affirmatively rather than adversarially.
The cases g=0,2,3,4 form a low-genus boundary range and are cited as affirmative cases with solvable points.
The genera 0, 2, 3, and 4 cases are low-genus boundary cases where the solvable-point question is affirmatively settled.
These are low-boundary genus cases with special low-degree maps, cited as positive solvability cases.
These are low-genus boundary/base cases with simpler curve structure, cited as positive cases rather than failures.
The genus 0,2,3,4 cases are affirmative small-genus boundary cases where special low-genus geometry gives solvable points.
The genus 0,2,3,4 cases are affirmative low-genus boundary cases rather than failures.
the g=0,2,3,4 cases form a low-genus boundary range with special low-degree maps and are used positively.
The cases of genus 0 and 2–4 are cited as positive low-genus boundary cases where solvable points are known.
The genus 0, 2, 3, and 4 cases are cited as positive low-genus boundary cases where solvable points exist.
They are cited as affirmative cases at the low-genus boundary rather than as failures of the claim.

examples.name: K5 stable curve with an S5-action
examples.note: The complete graph on five vertices, viewed as a graph of P^1s and descended through an S5 local-field extension, illustrates how non-number-field bases can have curves with no solvable points.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the K5 graph-of-P1s construction, checks stability from vertex degrees, adds an S5 Galois action, descends it, and explains how it yields a curve without points

passes.reason: singular K5-dual stable curve is a highly symmetric boundary degeneration used to build a no-point example
The K5 stable curve is a reducible boundary degeneration with a maximally complete dual graph and S5 symmetry, used in a construction where solvable points fail over other fields.
The K5 stable curve is a highly symmetric complete-graph nodal boundary degeneration used to build a no-solvable-points example over other fields.
The K5 curve is a boundary stable curve with maximal S5 symmetry and minimal-genus flavor in the construction, used to build a no-solvable-points obstruction over other fields.
The K5 dual graph gives a maximally connected, highly symmetric reducible stable degeneration used to produce a no-point obstruction.
The nodal union of P1s with complete K5 dual graph is a maximally edge-dense symmetric boundary curve used to construct no-solvable-point examples.
The K5 union of projective lines is a boundary stable curve, minimal for the S5 construction, and highly regular, used to build a no-solvable-points obstruction.
The K5 curve is a boundary stable degeneration built from a complete graph with high symmetry, used to produce a no-solvable-points obstruction.
the nodal K5 dual graph is a complete maximally symmetric boundary degeneration used to construct a curve with no points.
The K5 curve is a singular boundary stable curve built from the complete graph with full S5 symmetry and is used to construct fields with no solvable points.
The K5 curve is a maximally symmetric nodal stable degeneration of minimal genus for the S5 construction, used to build a no-solvable-point example over other fields.

examples.name: Locally soluble genus-one curve with no rational point
examples.note: A well-known genus-one curve whose Jacobian is an elliptic curve and which has points over every Q_p but not over Q makes concrete the distinction between elliptic curves and nontrivial genus-one torsors.
examples.engagement_reason: after introducing a locally soluble genus-one curve without rational points, the speaker works with such genus-one curves via their Jacobians, cohomology classes, descent/Selmer groups, and the solvable-points theorem

passes.reason: smooth genus-one torsor cited as a locally soluble failure of rational solvability
A locally soluble genus-one torsor with no rational point is structurally a non-sporadic genus-one curve, used as a failure of the local-to-global/rational-point expectation.
A locally soluble genus-one torsor with no Q-point is structurally non-sporadic but serves as a Hasse-principle counterexample.
A locally soluble genus-one curve without a rational point is an ordinary torsor-type genus-one curve used as a failure of the local-global/rational-point expectation.
A nontrivial genus-one torsor with points everywhere locally is used to refute the implication from local to rational points.
A nontrivial genus-one torsor in the Tate-Shafarevich group is used to show local solubility need not give a rational point.
A locally soluble genus-one curve without a rational point is used as a Hasse-principle failure and as a non-elliptic genus-one torsor.
A locally soluble genus-one curve with no rational point is a Hasse-principle counterexample but not structurally sporadic or degenerate.
a locally soluble genus-one torsor without a rational point is used as a Hasse-principle failure.
A locally soluble genus-one curve without a rational point is used as the standard Hasse-principle failure distinguishing genus-one curves from elliptic curves.
A locally soluble genus-one curve with no rational point is used as a Hasse-principle failure, but is structurally an ordinary nontrivial genus-one torsor.
